fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: A vintage-bike festival, not a conventional timed gran fondo, and part of the international Eroica family that began at Gaiole in Chianti in 1997. The Sunday Eroica Classic requires vintage or vintage-mechanism equipment (down-tube shifters and toe clips are the baseline; some modern components are permitted only when they meet the organizer's specified mechanical criteria) across four distances: KAKUSAN 50 (48.2 km), SUKESAN 100 (100.3 km), MITSUKUNI 150 (150.9 km), and SHOGUN 190 (185.9 km, rounded to 190 on the schedule). A separate Saturday REYNOLDS Ride opens the same three shorter distances to modern road, disc-brake, and compliant e-bikes. The 2026 edition relocates the event to Hitachinaka, Ibaraki Prefecture, from its previous Izu Peninsula home (2025).
course_description.signature_challenge: Completing SHOGUN 190 on vintage-mechanism equipment — mixed paved and unpaved roads, with down-tube shifting and toe clips as the baseline rather than modern indexed gearing.
suffering_zones: Mixed unpaved sections — Ibaraki Prefecture's own announcement confirms gravel/unpaved roads are part of the 2026 course
Equipment constraints as much as terrain — vintage-mechanism down-tube shifters and toe clips on any real gradient are a different discipline from modern indexed gearing
The Oarai-Hitachi seaside route sections, finishing toward the 'Mito 531' aid station

logistics.lodging_strategy: Onsen towns nearby offer traditional ryokans and hotels; book early for event weekend.

biased_opinion.summary: Eroica Japan is the real thing — a licensed outpost of the Eroica family that started in Tuscany in 1997, running in Japan since 2013 and marking its tenth anniversary in 2024. But this is fundamentally a vintage-bike festival, not a scored gran fondo: the Sunday Classic ride requires vintage-mechanism equipment (down-tube shifters and toe clips as the baseline, with only limited modern components permitted), and mixed paved/unpaved roads mean you're navigating real terrain on largely pre-indexed-shifting technology. The event has also moved location repeatedly — previously Izu Peninsula, relocating to Hitachinaka, Ibaraki for 2026.
strengths: Genuine connection to the original L'Eroica lineage (Gaiole in Chianti, 1997) — not an imitation using the name
Ten-plus years running in Japan (since 2013), with a real festival atmosphere around vintage cycling culture
Four distance options (48-190 km) on the vintage Classic ride, plus a separate modern-bike Saturday ride for riders without period equipment
Confirmed mixed-surface course — genuine terrain variety, not a closed-loop pavement circuit
weaknesses: Not a timed, scored race — this is a vintage-equipment festival ride, so competitive riders looking for a ranked result should look elsewhere
Location changes between editions (Izu Peninsula historically, Hitachinaka/Ibaraki for 2026) — course character is not stable year to year

Vintage-equipment rules are a real barrier: no refunds after entry, and a mandatory bicycle inspection (¥4,400 without a pre-approved inspection sheet) adds cost and logistics most gran fondos don't require
No published field size or entrant cap found — hard to gauge event scale in advance
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuine, well-pedigreed vintage-cycling pilgrimage rather than a competitive gran fondo — go for the equipment culture and the mixed-terrain course, not for a race result. Verify the current year's location directly with the organizer before booking travel; it moves.

final_verdict.one_liner: Iconic vintage road event blending Japanese onsen culture with Eroica's retro prestige.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if vintage-bike culture and a genuine Eroica-family event appeal to you and you own or can source period-correct equipment. Skip if you want a timed, competitive result — this is a festival ride, not a race.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want the original vintage-bike event: L'Eroica (Gaiole in Chianti, Italy). If you want modern-bike gravel in Japan without the vintage-equipment requirement, look for Japan's other gravel gran fondos.
